title: Part 14
text: to master his human imperfections, his pride and his self-love. Hobbes’ solution was to tame the passions with the Leviathan, agreed upon by fear-driven individuals. Only in this way would natural man become civilized, and only in this way would peace once again prevail. The Hobbesian Person If Hobbes’ philosophical anthropology seems overly simplified and openly functional to his following analysis, his reduction of the self must nonetheless be taken seriously, for after some initial strong criticism, the Hobbesian person did become foundational in political matters. The Hobbesian view of human nature 12 In his presentation of Hobbes, Voegelin makes the dubious (and undocumented) statement that the biblical symbol of the Leviathan had been designed by an English thinker in direct response to the Puritan danger (Voegelin 2000: 240). Martinich (1992: 362) follows the more generally-accepted suggestion that Hobbes designed the frontispiece himself. 13 Descartes criticized Hobbes’ work on precisely these grounds. Commenting on Hobbes’ De Cive of 1642, Descartes remarked that Hobbes’ could have achieved his goals better by adopting more ‘solid and virtuous principles’ than those upon which he in fact grounded his case (Descartes in Sommerville 1992: 3). To Descartes, Hobbes’ principles of human nature were ‘very wicked and very dangerous, in that he supposes that all men are evil, or that he gives them reason to be so’ (Descartes in ibid.: 41). Hobbes, conversely, saw nothing constructive in Descartes’ arguments for scepticism (Williams 1998: 48), as he had made clear in his objections to Descartes’ Meditations on First Philosophy in 1641. However, and as discussed by Tuck (2002), these mutual dismissals must also be understood as comments made by two jealous rivals competing in their geometrical search for the first principles of the new science. Liminality and the Modern 130 still exerts great influence on political theory today, most visibly within various schools of rational choice theory and the realist paradigm in international relations. It was connected to a larger world-view. John Ruggie claims that on the level of social metaphysics, modern territorial rulers from the seventeenth century started to view society as ‘a collection of atomistic and autonomous bodies-in-motion in a field of forces energized solely by scarcity and ambition’ (Ruggie 1993: 158). The autonomous state of Hobbes rested upon selfish passion-driven individuals, whose order rested in and upon the polity itself. The possibility of peacefully keeping together a society thus came to reside in the negative definition of the human person. While this is a very different reduction of the self when compared with the narrowing of being established in the Cartesian system, the effects of the two constructs can reinforce each other: the rational order that follows in both cases establishes a specifically empty and delimited understanding of the human personality – a view which in all other historical periods would have stood no chance of becoming accepted (see Szakolczai 2011). The construct Hobbes was after must cut off both ‘nature’ and ‘divinity’, personal belief and transcendental truth, in one stroke. Society should be held together, peace and order secured, by a civil theology whose indisputable truth resided within itself, which it itself represented, and which could not be brought to judgment by any external source of power but itself. Human nature and God’s order would then come together as one, both ordained by the Sovereign. 14 Reduced to a selfish animal, the Hobbesian person became authentically human only through the higher reality of the Sovereign. 15 The Sovereign, in his turn, represented pure order. He, symbolizing the state, was the ordered Cartesian self of politics from whom truth flows. Although in Leviathan Hobbes very explicitly discerned God’s role in society from that of the Sovereign, at a deeper level this means that the Sovereign comes to stand in the same kind of direct contact with God, such that truth and the order of society simply are God himself. 14 The minor weakness of this seemingly perfect system, of which Hobbes was pragmatically aware, was that the problem of passion-blind, power-thirsty individuals was duplicated at the inter-state level since each state was a self-enclosed, power-maximizing unit pitted against every other in a continuous state of war. War was from that perspective the normal and expected state of affairs, peace just a prelude to another war. Between and over states there was, and should not be, any supreme Sovereign. This being so, international peace accords would come to represent the temporary absence of war. In practice this principle became accepted. Much later it was built into modern political geography when Ratzel (1897) claimed that states would most naturally seek territorial expansion, and that only a fear of defeat would hold them back. The realist paradigm in international relations theory always took this Hobbesian point of view as an unquestioned premise. 15 Hobbes’ definition of the person goes with his theory of representation and runs as follows: ‘A person, is he, whose words or actions are considered, either as his own, or as representing the words and actions of another man, or of any other thing’. The natural person is no good to Hobbes. The good and civil person is he who ceases to exist as natural by fusing into the higher reality of the Leviathan. Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 131 We should understand this construct well, for in Voegelin’s reading this is critically what underlies the gnostic nature of the Hobbesian state, and which renders meaning to the very term ‘absolute’. Importantly, Hobbes did not rule out religion or nature. In fact, as is often overlooked, a major part of Leviathan is dedicated to a purely theological discussion, and in this sense Hobbes writes well within a theological paradigm. Hobbes wanted to bring Christianity down to earth, and he does so by interpreting the Bible against Greek philosophy, which was charged with having ‘unduly spiritualized Christianity’ (Pacchi 2002: 185). To Hobbes, divine law and natural law were to function only insofar as they respected the social laws, the truth of society. In Hobbes’ model the mortal God fused into the immortal God, or as the title states, his Leviathan was a Commonwealth both ‘Civil and Ecclesiastical’. A theoretical point of a general relevance follows from this. European modernity as it developed from the seventeenth century is normally understood as a separation of secular from sacred. In most prevailing theories, European modernity is characterized by secularization of the diverse sub-fields of human thought and action. This separation of sacred and secular can then be identified with the rise of the secular polity, as it grew out of the absolute state. On the one hand, there is an obvious element of truth in these accounts. Scientific rationalization, abstraction and homogenization of time and space and new techniques of measuring and calculating were indeed developments followed by political rationalization and bureaucratization, epitomized in the centralist, absolutist state. Yet the common view of political modernity as a process of differentiation of politics from religion must be modified, for what happens is also quite the opposite. The historical novelty of Hobbes’ construct is rather that state and Church come to stand in such a close relationship that any contradiction or tension between temporal and spiritual becomes meaningless (Voegelin 2000: 53). The political model of Hobbes – that was to become the model of the modern state, sealing itself off against other modern states – did not separate out divinity. It took away its autonomy by standing so close to it that it could draw directly from its primary truth. Hobbes’ model was peculiar, not for clearing the ground by separating sacred from secular, but for bringing them so close that the tension ceased to be meaningful on the level of thought and social organization. If this is a process of ‘secularization’, it is so only insofar as it manages to hide its own origins. 16 The Illusion of ‘Absolutism’? We have argued so far that Descartes and Hobbes both created a distinct and novel type of ‘absolutism’ in the form of a strongly demarcated boundary around 16 Or, as Pizzorno (1987) argues, if absolute politics were indeed based on the historical dissociation of the spiritual and the temporal realms, this process should not be confounded with what is generally seen as a process of secularization of politics. Liminality and the Modern 132 either self or state, with strong claims to self-foundation and with parallel types of symbolism. At this point, the objection to the above account of statehood and personhood might be that the idea of absolutism was a myth from the beginning: it never ‘happened’, either within thought, or within political rule. Indeed, such a criticism has been part and parcel of modern philosophy since Descartes. The possible objection is therefore that the analysis suggested neglects the fierce critiques of Cartesian rationalism. Did not Pascal blame Descartes for ‘wanting to do without God’, and was not this reaction constitutive of modernity? This is not the question but the answer. Within the interpretative space of modernity (Wagner 2008), critiques could be raised only insofar as they set themselves against the project of rationality. Similar objections have been launched against Hobbes’ model of human nature. Montesquieu and Rousseau had an easy time showing that Hobbes’ man was indeed nothing but a peculiar history- specific image transferred to the level of humanity. ‘Nature’ or ‘culture’ in some form or the other were then launched against Hobbesian or Cartesian rationality. Rationality became something to hate or love, attack or defend. Rousseau aimed to show that nature could not be kept out of self and society, or that it ought not be kept out. Freud argued that Culture without libido equalled repression of the Id (his version of human nature) and led to an unbalanced Ego. Such attempts to counter ‘too much rationality’, however, share the predictable outcome that they reinforce the divide they set out to overcome. This is especially so as the natural or cultural forces they set loose against the forces of reason are in themselves quite ‘un-ordered’, with no real alternative to the excess of order. What unites Pascal, Rousseau, Nietzsche, Freud and most other sceptics of modernity is that they proposed a new kind of model in which ‘passions’, ‘sentiments’, ‘will- power’, or ‘drives’, would once again enter the world. However, these celebrations of rule-transcending self-assertion or boundless self-expression would never succeed in creating any real connection to reflexive self-formation through self- restraint upon which society could be positively re-imagined. The reduced and impoverished self left behind by Descartes and Hobbes would remain as a residual category, re-injected with passions of whatever kind. However effective these critiques were, and however much they jointly came to model modernity and the modern personality, the kind of self they set out to defend could not possibly serve as a new source of a positive order – as the rationalists would easily respond. And so the discussion reached a stalemate. The mainstream debates that characterized the 1980s and 1990s between postmodernists and modernists only served to underline that fact. In similar ways, the absolute model of the state has undergone criticism since its inception, and not only from a religious perspective. It has today become increasingly frequent to question the sovereignty of the European state as it came into being from the Peace of Westphalia in 1648. Political theorists have shown how in practice the autonomy of the state was never absolute (see for example Krasner 1995). In the present period where the very concept of state sovereignty Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 133 is seriously brought into question, this tendency to de-absolutize the state back into history is understandably widespread. State power remained inwardly and outwardly contested from 1648, relying on international power configurations, then as now. The argument can be pressed by showing that with the very rise of absolutism the state was in fact becoming increasingly ruled by the logics of a globalizing economy. From this perspective, absolutism was the (false) ideology of state power that hid the reality of economic globalization, which spread precisely from the sixteenth century. In the argument of Wallerstein (1980), absolutism was invented precisely when absolute power had become a historical impossibility. Neither of these criticisms, however important, disqualify what we try to argue here. That we cannot construct ourselves from cosmic exile, and that states, then as now, remained context-dependent systems of power is so obviously true that we have to wonder why the idea of self-enclosed selves and states was ever invented in the first place. This question, however, can hardly be answered by examining any real ‘substance’ of self and state, or by demonstrating externals such as nature, culture, un-reason, globalization, or any other type of polluting otherness. To answer the question we can instead look to the succession of forms that the concepts of state and self have taken, consider the conditions of their emergence, and their long term effects. The idea of self-autonomy may be a fiction, but it remains a highly powerful one. The Shared Conditions of Emergence: Early Modernity as Liminal Period In other words, if the logic of the absolute state and self stands in contradiction to practice and common-sense, and possibly even to the logics of its own argument (the ‘Cartesian circle’ corresponds closely to the Hobbesian contract made by men already willing to make the contract; they are a view from nowhere), we must place it against the problem it was meant to solve. The problem that had become unbearable during the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries has already been outlined above. It was a religious crisis, originating in the waning of the Middle Ages, and it touched all the basic relations between God and man, society and nature. Protestantism, puritan and revivalist as it was, it became one answer to the dissolution of order. Following the religious schisms, Europe entered a century and half of continuous warfare. This warfare took on international dimensions, but led also to ceaseless civil wars within the European polities, as in France and England, the birthplaces of Descartes and Hobbes. The Hobbesian problem of order was therefore not some distant abstract, philosophical preoccupation, neither was the general shattering of truth that worried Descartes. Both Hobbes and Descartes developed their thoughts during the Thirty Years’ War. The continued existence of war and unrest was their ‘normal’ background experience. Historians will continue to discuss whether these wars were really religious in character or whether struggles over religious doctrine mostly served as a pretext for the reorganization of power. From the Liminality and the Modern 134 perspective provided here, the important fact to stress is that the wars and the social and political revolutions of the seventeenth century were typically liminal in character. Arguments over religious doctrine may therefore have served as cover for wider power struggles, without altering the socio-religious character of the larger crisis and the answers given to it. Truths about the world which were so obviously culturally and historically determined were elevated to absolute truths and employed by all involved parties to claim their just cause, and to draw the sword in the name of that justice. Without any ordered centre of ‘truth-power’, this slippery use of truth and reason had become mimetically dangerous. It had to stop. Some kind of first principle for truth-measurement and power-use was required. The search for a principle of equality, for an overall figure or notion of judgment with an undifferentiated measure of justice and truth was equally fundamental to Descartes and Hobbes. It was a quest of their age. The novelty of the warfare that plagued Europe from the sixteenth century was not only its temporal duration, but also its increasing involvement of the human masses. Hobbes was fearful of the civil wars that managed to drag the masses into strife by tying the personally felt truth of the believer to the re-organization of power. This emotional involvement of the masses, and the uncontrollable situations it led to, even within singular polities, represented something new and frightening in European history. If, as proposed here, such an age can be seen as a period of liminality, the crystallization of ideas that took place during this period must be given special attention. Following van Gennep’s tripartite structure, once the liminal period is closed, the ideas and practices that have been established will settle as new form and take on the quality of structure. History in such a view is not a continuous stream of action governed by a structure that changes only slowly, if at all. Rather, there are historical moments in which structure is loose, and there are other moments in which structure takes on the quality of doxa, where it becomes frozen. Drawing on the anthropological understanding of ritual liminality, the playfulness of the period of liminality is at one and the same time unstructured and highly structuring: the most basic rules of behaviour are questioned, doubt and scepticism as to the existence of the world are radicalized, but the problematizations, the formative experiences and the reformulations of being during the period of liminality proper will feed the individual (and his or her cohort) with a new structure and set of rules that, once established, will glide back to the level of the taken-for-granted. This follows Weber’s approach to the analysis of ideas and ‘images’, closely linked to their rationalization and systematization by intellectuals. Voegelin’s analysis of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries is in fact quite in line with this interpretation, and so are his conceptual terms. He calls the period from the early sixteenth century to the middle of the seventeenth century ‘The Great Confusion’, the subtitles given to Volumes IV (Renaissance and Reformation) and V (Religion and Modernity) in his History of Political Ideas manuscripts. The disorder created by the Lutheran schism, and the controversy and revolution that followed, produced a variety of attempts at restoring intellectual order as it Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 135 became increasingly clear that a restoration of scholastic modes of thought in politics and science could not cope with the situation (Voegelin 1998: 17–28). The period remained a ‘fluid field of sentiments and ideas’, until a new systematic view of man and the world took shape, and this ‘crystallization into systematic form takes place only in the seventeenth century’ (ibid.: 134). Applying the concept of liminality to large-scale societies undergoing particularly stressful periods, it can be suggested that two main dimensions of the crisis of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries were singled out – by single personalities, and by a larger filtering process, here only hinted at – as particularly pressing, and these dimensions would come to be understood as the real reasons of the crisis: there was the problem of knowledge about the world (and the role of bodily perception in the process of knowledge), which would establish itself as the central problem of modern philosophy; and there was the problem of social order, which would become the main preoccupation of rule and of political science. The two problems are indeed typical founding challenges and archetypical themes in liminality, as shown by human experiences of ritual passages. But it is also clear that alternative problematizations could and did in fact exist. 17 Moreover, both formulations would secure, from opposite ends, not only the success of ‘absolutism’, but also the future viability of alternative models. Such alternative models would, paradoxically, be required to solve a problem whose very formulation had come to be taken for granted: how the closed human self as mind could reach knowledge and construct a system from its own grounds, and how the ‘unordered’ and power-thirsty self could find social peace through the rational Sovereign. It was exactly this formulation, this problematization, which became part and parcel of the very problem that it kept producing. Lasting Effects: Orders and Borders of Modern State and Self It is often asked what role the organism analogy has played in the social and political sciences: in what ways does a society resemble (the functioning of) the human person? The question can be inverted: what is the preconceived idea of 17 Voegelin’s sympathy for Jean Bodin as a representative of a ‘Mediterranean modernity’, seen as an alternative to what he termed an ‘Atlantic modernity’ (the Dutch- French-British modernity based on the scientism and secularity of the seventeenth century) must certainly be understood in this way. Bodin recognized the end of the Christian mediaeval worldview and constructed a complete theory of politics. However, Bodin theorized the national state using analogies from a cosmic order and hierarchy, and Bodin’s entire cosmology was still inspired by the richness of the Platonic and Hellenistic traditions, contemplating the order of the soul and its role in the polis, carefully avoiding the materialistic metaphysics of the Hobbesian system (Voegelin discusses Bodin in two different places of his (abandoned) manuscripts that were published in the volume entitled Religion and the Rise of Modernity (1998: 158–68; 180–251), volume V of his History of Political Ideas). It goes without saying that Hobbes read Bodin very selectively. Liminality and the Modern 136 the human person that it can be mapped metaphorically onto state and society? In a Cartesian universe, the individualist self is an autonomous, self-organizing, self-motivated being, who should be able to control the boundaries of his external world well; what modern medical standards would call insanity occurs when he does not know how to handle them. With Descartes this capacity of boundary- drawing became the very possibility of the reason and rationality upon which modernity was epistemologically, scientifically and institutionally built. Divinity enters this self insofar as it follows and respects the truth of logical reason. As suggested above, divinity becomes one with the truth of the self as reasoning mind. Moving our attention to historically shifting concepts of state, it can, along parallel lines, be suggested that differences in state organization may be thought of in terms of the kinds of borders drawn around the polity. Modern states can simply be defined as states with absolute borders within which they exercise absolute power (Thomassen 2005). By such standards, a threat to state borders amounts to a questioning of its reason for being and a declaration of war. From the seventeenth century states became conceptualized as self-organized, self-motivated beings that operate from their own set of rules, and from a self-foundational rationality, fittingly termed reason of state. Divinity enters the political organization of society insofar as it follows and respects the truth of society. Divinity becomes one with the truth of society. It is in this sense that the Hobbesian Sovereign has a clear parallel in the sovereignty of Descartes’ self. It is reason upon fear against reason upon mind. Yet both thinkers postulated a form of first principle from which truths about the world could be generated from obvious premises. Descartes’ system departed from the cogito, while Hobbes’ single axiom was that of self-preservation. The order they bestowed upon the world would have different effects in different domains, rationalizing thought and governance respectively. And yet the Hobbesian reason of state is structurally similar, almost down to the last detail, to the Cartesian reason of self. Historically and conceptually, the sovereign state came into being with the absolute self. The problem became one of safeguarding borders and boundaries, to keep out, and then to concentrate, order and centralize the energies of the interior. Such perceived security and freedom were obtained, for both state and self, through preserving integrity, through avoiding outside interference, and defending and preserving absolute borders (Neack and Knudson 1996). The very possibility of order turned into a question of peace and security of the interior for both state and self, but in that process the quality of that interior was continuously pre-empted. The human need to mediate passions and drives through an experience of transcendence was (ill)recognized as the very source of disorder. Arguably, this misrecognition of spirituality as dangerous passion would only inflame the danger it had set out to tame, creating a vicious circle: absolute power was erected as a barrier to protect against self-asserting individuals, but as such it also kept producing them. From the analysis offered, we can start to pin down what might be so problematic about seventeenth century modernity – our modernity. In the search for Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 137 a ‘first principle’, both Hobbes and Descartes found their answer in a real liminal experience: universal fear and endless doubt. The particularity of their construct, however, was that instead of replacing that fear or doubt with the establishment of society on the basis of a new normality, they turned these liminal experiences par excellence into the new building blocks of the new reality. As Voegelin said it, the search for the ground thus remains recognizable as the reality experienced even in the modes of deformation. Hobbes and Descartes are liminal thinkers in this very Weberian sense: they departed from an ‘image of the world’, and then ‘took a stand in the face of the world’ by rationalizing the image and devising a system from it. They recognized and entered liminality by declaring the world radically and finally alien to the self. At the same time, from that experience of liminality, from that experience of fear and diabolic doubt, standing against the world, they proposed a fundament and a way out. Hobbes and Descartes took us through liminality and ended it by establishing a new order; but they did so by establishing liminality at its core. Such a foundation will always have a problem with its own limits: it is inherently prone to that hubris and that excess that the ancient Greeks had recognized as a real danger, and which the Platonic soul was meant to counter. While Hobbes’ and Descartes’ continued attacks on the ancients and Aristotelianism were very explicit attempts to overcome mediaeval thinking, one could equally well claim that what Hobbes and Descartes jointly succeeded in doing was to complete the undoing of the (Italian) Renaissance ideals. They did so by definitively tearing apart the two threads that Renaissance thinking had carefully tried to recover and unite: the life and teaching of Christ (with a stress on Jesus as an exemplary prophet) and Greek philosophy (with a stress on the unity between truth, beauty and order). In Renaissance philosophy, those were the threads that could sustain a new-found subjectivity (Szakolczai 2007). In this vein, the Enlightenment period that developed a century later simply confirmed that separation, although with a different stress. While seventeenth century philosophy filtered away the Hellenic inspiration, the nineteenth century Enlightenment did the same with Christianity, despiritualizing Hellenic thought exactly as Hobbes and Descartes had despiritualized Christianity. The standard reference to the Renaissance as a forerunner to the Enlightenment is on that account simply not tenable. The secularization that followed from the Cartesian and Hobbesian systems of thought is paradoxical, for neither Hobbes nor Descartes intended the rational systems they devised to refute divinity; quite the contrary. One can indeed argue that both Descartes and Hobbes wanted to safeguard Christianity, and in a very similar way: by making it congruent with a new scientific theory; indeed, by making God’s existence (Descartes) and the Holy Scriptures (Hobbes) the foundation of a new scientific worldview. Descartes had no desire to rule out the transcendental realm. Proving God’s existence was a crucial matter to him. But he claimed God existed insofar – and only insofar – as it was revealed through the truth of the self as mind. The knowing mind is therefore not Godless (as Descartes would have to Liminality and the Modern 138 reassure his troubled Christian public). It stands in the closest proximity to God himself. It stands indeed so close that any contradiction between God and Pure Knowledge becomes meaningless. The conditions of pure reason and truth of the Cartesian self are God himself. They are the same. As it turns out, this is not a separation of divinity from the sphere of knowledge, leaving the religious sphere autonomous but without influence in the this-worldly realm. Rather, it is a total control of divinity – and in that sense a total destruction. What is carried out here is not a separation of politics from a religious context. It is rather ‘the elimination of the life of the spirit from public representation and the corresponding contraction of politics to a secular nucleus’ (Voegelin 1998: 23, italics added). As Hobbes did from the perspective of the state, so Descartes accomplished from the perspective of the self. Knowledge should be reached through pure reason whose indisputable truth resided within itself, which it itself represented, and that could not be brought to judgment by any external source of power but itself. The Hobbesian state and the Cartesian self are on this note strikingly similar in attributes, and suggest a correspondence between our concepts of state and self which is much more than incidental. Despite its secularizing effects on thought and governance, this commonality can best be grasped by staying within its original religious discourse: the common ground for the absolute self and the absolute state was the final reversal of the structure of revelation. From this analysis, a different understanding of ‘absolutism’ becomes possible. Reversing the structure of revelation did not imply that the transcendental realm ceased to exist but rather that it would take on new forms. Kant’s transcendental subject is transcendental because it pulls itself up by its own bootstraps, not because it finds its necessary condition of being in a divinely ordained order of nature: it is transcendence through detachment, and from that detachment order is bestowed on nature, and from that quality of order-bestowing its divinity is established. Man is divine through his ordering capacities. Similarly, the structure of the apparently a-religious political revolutions that swept Europe from the nineteenth century carried on the structure of transcendence just as well, only that the transcendent was now brought down to install a this-worldly future-orientation: a ‘horizontal transcendence’, to use another
